item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you very much for the nice comment!

Second, nice-ass image, don't know if it's a 'shop or not.

It's a shop! Leveritas did the SCP logo on the guy's shirt.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ok first, incredibly well written with an interesting concept. Second, nice-ass image, don't know if it's a 'shop or not.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Both these links look good to me!

(I think there are inconsistencies in the articles that would make this a poor crosslink, but I would also like to point out that the whale carcass D-7███ finds is intended to be a very subtle reference to SCP-1128.)

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: I like it! Of course, if my co-author prefers differently, I will defer to them.

item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Interesting. I have two potential links to propose. First off, the mention during the final addendum of the "standard Humanoid Containment Cell" can be tweaked minorly to refer to a "Type S Standard Humanoid Anomaly Containment Cell" and link out to Reach's Blueprints where a lot of these terms are becoming standardized and visualized. Additionally, the anomaly here, in which extradimensional aquatic creatures enter our world via bodies of water, is reminiscent of SCP-791. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

D-5███ had been presumed killed in action following the events of SCP-3374 exploration; however, at approximately 3:14 AM on March 16, two days after the exploration, local residents reported a malnourished and severely dehydrated individual anomalously manifesting in their backyard pool. MTF Gamma-7 operatives amnesticized the residents and extracted the individual, later confirmed to be D-5███. Although recovering quickly after medical treatment, D-5███ was found after testing to consistently demonstrate an anomalous ability to phase through solid structures in water, congruous with abilities of SCP-3374-1 instances. D-5███ accordingly reclassified as SCP-3374-2 and placed in a standard Humanoid Containment Cell within the Wakoe County Public Recreation Complex.

To this:

D-5███ had been presumed killed in action following the events of SCP-3374 exploration; however, at approximately 3:14 AM on March 16, two days after the exploration, local residents reported a malnourished and severely dehydrated individual anomalously manifesting in their backyard pool. MTF Gamma-7 operatives amnesticized the residents and extracted the individual, later confirmed to be D-5███. Although recovering quickly after medical treatment, D-5███ was found after testing to consistently demonstrate an anomalous ability to phase through solid structures in water, congruous with abilities of SCP-3374-1 instances. D-5███ accordingly reclassified as SCP-3374-2 and placed in a Type S Standard Humanoid Anomaly Containment Cell within the Wakoe County Public Recreation Complex.

Changing this:

SCP-3374 is a phenomenon affecting bodies of water in the city of Wakoe, Illinois. As of currently, SCP-3374 manifests at locations within a roughly circular area encompassing the majority of the city. Affected bodies of water will exhibit the presence of a wide range of irregular aquatic organisms (designated SCP-3374-1). Many SCP-3374-1 instances do not resemble any form of known terrestrial life, and will enter and exit the affected body of water seemingly regardless of any natural or man-made boundaries around the water.

To this:

SCP-3374 is a phenomenon affecting bodies of water in the city of Wakoe, Illinois. As of currently, SCP-3374 manifests at locations within a roughly circular area encompassing the majority of the city. Affected bodies of water will exhibit the presence of a wide range of irregular aquatic organisms (designated SCP-3374-1). Many SCP-3374-1 instances do not resemble any form of known terrestrial life, and will enter and exit the affected body of water seemingly regardless of any natural or man-made boundaries around the water.1

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

Footnotes
1. Similarity between instances of SCP-3374-1 and organisms exiting SCP-791 during a "Draining Event" have been observed. Investigation into a potential conection between the two anomalies is ongoing.

item.title: Re: D-Class Names
item.content:encoded:
In-universe: pretty much what Zyn said. I like to think that the Foundation has at least decent privacy protocols, so in this case it's extended to D-Class.

Out-of-universe: it's a lot easier to quickly differentiate between D-5███ and D-7███ than it is to differentiate between D-5923 and D-7448. It's a bit of a handwave, sure, but I think it does enough to help the flow of the narrative to justify its inclusion.

Glad you enjoyed!

item.title: Re: D-Class Names
item.content:encoded: I think it was to indicate that the identities were sensitive information. Quantum Physician probably has a better answer, though.

item.title: D-Class Names
item.content:encoded:
Out of curiosity, why are the d-class designations blacked out?

Still a fun read, just wondering.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Five lights … giant opabinia?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mentioning it would be very weird coming from myself, haha! Thanks for noticing and you're welcome!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, QP and I had some trouble figuring out a good balance of detail and basics. We'd revised the description a bunch of times in response to "too little"/"too much" being told to us in the editing stage. Not too sure what exactly to do about the clunkiness, but we'll see about some alternate phrasings I guess.

Thanks for the +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1. This was a lot of fun.

On the critique side, I think the description was somewhat clunky, in that there was a lot of text spent trying to describe the effects and specific technical details and not much of it providing a clear mental image. I only got a solid idea what the phasing meant after reading several examples.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ffffft I just noticed that we didn't credit you for the image edit!! Let me remedy that immediately.

And thanks again for the help. :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad to have helped out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, they redacted a whole county to keep this contained! :O

Really fun stuff. The effect by itself is interesting, but the exploration log makes it. :D

item.title: Re: Dive Depth
item.content:encoded: I've added in a couple of lines addressing it at the beginning of the exploration log. Let me know if that helps.

item.title: Super Fun Read
item.content:encoded: I had a smile on my face pretty much the whole time I was reading this. I was a little worried the whole thing was going to get too hokey when the barrier appeared, but there was enough restraint to keep it out of true cheese territory. It was a nice touch. I also especially enjoyed 2004's manifestation. The description definitely deserved the chuckle I let out. +1.

item.title: Re: Dive Depth
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I'll mention that to QP and we'll figure out an edit for this. Thanks for the suggestion!

item.title: Re: Dive Depth
item.content:encoded:
That doesn't explain why they didn't experience the effects of the water pressure,

Simply put, the "water" in that dimension is different.

or why the original planned dive was still 3 times that of the deepest dive ever.

I think there might have been more of an indication in previous drafts that this exploration log was done after a fair amount of testing indicated that it would be safe for humans to enter the dimension and stay until those depths, but it might have gotten trimmed out. Quantum and I can add some details to make that more obvious.

Also, to be clear, I do still like this article, and I am upvoting it.

Thanks!

item.title: Re: Dive Depth
item.content:encoded: In that case, maybe there should be a note that the cage had a pressure sensor that would automatically stop the winch if it went to deep, but which never activated because it never got bad enough.

item.title: Re: Dive Depth
item.content:encoded:
That doesn't explain why they didn't experience the effects of the water pressure, or why the original planned dive was still 3 times that of the deepest dive ever.

Also, to be clear, I do still like this article, and I am upvoting it.

item.title: Re: Dive Depth
item.content:encoded:
Spoilers?
hide

The water area that the D-Class were diving into is not on Earth.

item.title: Dive Depth
item.content:encoded: 3.5 km is a nigh impossible depth for someone using only scuba gear. At depths much lower than that, the pressure can cause the air the person is breathing to become toxic. Nitrogen can induce an intoxicated state at just 30 meters. Oxygen can become acutely toxicity at 60 meters. Even inert helium, used by divers to dilute their air mixtures, can cause convulsions at 150 meters. The deepest anyone has dived using scuba gear is 332 meters.

item.title: Re: Author Post – Hey, a thing with fish in it!
item.content:encoded:
I had a lot of fun writing this, especially since at some point while the draft was in the works (or possibly before the co-authorship was begun) I acquired two betta fish I love vewwy vewwy much and I really liked the concept QP had. He did a great job on the exploration log and finding a cool image for the ending.

Hope you all enjoy reading! This was quite an adventure to be part of.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, some people in chat mentioned that. Apparently pressing Shift + F5 on the page might fix it?

ETA: I believe Wikipedia has the answer! https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Bypass_your_cache

It looks alright to me at the moment, so it might be a WikiDot error.

Thanks very much for the +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The picture seems to be broken.
Otherwise, this is pretty good. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I very much enjoyed the fact that the exploration log didn't include a ton of dialogue in addition to the interesting details. Everything was to the point, with just the right amount of weirdness. Also, the fact that something called the Wakoe County Public Recreation Complex now houses a human prisoner is incredibly amusing.

+1

item.title: Author Post – Hey, a thing with fish in it!
item.content:encoded:
This has been floating around in my head, on the forums, and in a sandbox for over 6 months now, so it's been a long time coming. With that in mind, there's a lot of people I have to thank for getting this thing onto the site:

First and foremost, Zyn, my fantastic co-author and wise Foundation sensei. She saw potential in the bare-bones summary of the draft I first posted and took this in ways I'd never considered, but which I can definitely see now helped elevate this whole thing beyond "ooooohhh there is a shark in a swimming pool isn't it scary". For helping to make this into an actual SCP and not just an idea, Zyn: thank you.

Modern_Erasmus, Sly161, Mr Abadede, Tuomey Tombstone, and Ashkenazic, thanks for some catching all the inconsistencies and leaps of logic that we didn't. You guys are great.

Image is from the U.S. Navy's Military Sealift Command, and therefore should be under Creative Commons as per their image use policy. Image edit was done by Leveritas.

Hope you enjoy. Anyone who spots the in-article references gets a prize. ;)
